attributes.stem: A 30-year-old man presents to his General Practice clinic with a three-month history of recurrent pain, swelling, and redness around his right great toenail. He works as a carpenter and enjoys playing soccer on weekends. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The lateral nail fold of his right great toe is swollen, erythematous, and tender to touch, with purulent discharge visible (Image). The condition has not improved despite previous courses of oral antibiotics and proper foot hygiene.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management for this condition?
attributes.explanation:
The image and clinical description are consistent with chronic paronychia secondary to an ingrown toenail. The most appropriate management in this case is wedge excision of toenail. This procedure involves removing the ingrown portion of the nail along with the associated nail matrix, which prevents recurrence. It is particularly indicated in cases of chronic paronychia that have not responded to conservative measures, as seen in this patient who has had recurrent issues despite previous treatments.

Topical antifungal cream would be appropriate for fungal nail infections or tinea pedis, but it is not the primary treatment for chronic paronychia caused by an ingrown toenail. While fungal infections can complicate ingrown toenails, addressing the underlying structural issue is crucial.

Oral antibiotics for two weeks may be helpful in acute cases or as an adjunct to other treatments, but alone they are unlikely to resolve chronic paronychia caused by an ingrown toenail. This patient has already tried antibiotics without long-term success, indicating the need for a more definitive solution.

Silver nitrate application is sometimes used in the treatment of hypergranulation tissue associated with chronic wounds or excessive granulation tissue around the nail fold. However, it does not address the underlying cause of the ingrown toenail and is not the most appropriate first-line treatment for chronic paronychia.

Punch biopsy of lesion would be considered if there was suspicion of malignancy or an atypical presentation. However, in this case, the clinical presentation is typical for chronic paronychia due to an ingrown toenail, and a biopsy is not indicated as the first step in management.

It's worth noting that the patient's occupation as a carpenter and hobby of playing soccer may contribute to the development and persistence of ingrown toenails due to repetitive trauma to the toes. Patient education about proper nail care and footwear may be beneficial in preventing future occurrences.
learningPoints: Chronic paronychia from an ingrown toenail is best managed with wedge excision of the toenail.
Occupations involving repetitive trauma to the toes, such as carpentry, can predispose individuals to ingrown toenails and chronic paronychia.
Conservative treatments are often insufficient for chronic paronychia caused by ingrown toenails.
references: Bryant A, Knox A. Ingrown toenails: the role of the GP. Australian Family Physician. 2015;44(3):102-105. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2015/march/ingrown-toenails-the-role-of-the-gp

RAGCP. Partial nail avulsion and matricectomy for ingrown toenails. 2016. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/handi/handi-interventions/procedures/partial-nail-avulsion-and-matricectomy-for-ingrown
